title: Quiz: Quantum Policy Networks
description: Test your understanding of quantum circuits as policy networks for RL

questions.question: What is the typical pipeline for a quantum policy?

options.text: State encoding → Variational circuit → Measurement → Action probabilities
Variational circuit → State encoding → Action → Measurement

questions.explanation: A quantum policy encodes the state into qubits, applies a parameterized variational circuit, measures to get probabilities, and maps these to action probabilities.

questions.question: What is angle encoding?

options.text: Encoding state values as amplitudes of quantum states
Encoding state values as rotation angles (e.g., RY(s_i))

questions.explanation: Angle encoding uses rotation gates like RY(π·s_i) to encode each state feature as a rotation angle on a corresponding qubit.

questions.question: How are action probabilities typically extracted from a quantum policy?

options.text: By measuring and mapping bitstring probabilities to actions
By counting the number of gates

questions.explanation: Action probabilities come from measuring the quantum state (|ψ|²) and mapping measurement outcomes (bitstrings) to action indices.

questions.question: What is data re-uploading in quantum policies?

options.text: Re-encoding the state at each variational layer
Uploading data to a quantum cloud

questions.explanation: Data re-uploading (repeated encoding) interleaves state encoding with variational layers, potentially increasing the expressiveness of the quantum circuit.

questions.question: For quantum policy gradients, we can use the parameter-shift rule to compute ∂π(a|s)/∂θ.
questions.explanation: True. The parameter-shift rule computes gradients of quantum observables/probabilities with respect to circuit parameters, enabling policy gradient computation.

questions.question: How can a quantum policy handle continuous action spaces?

options.text: By using expectation values ⟨Z⟩ mapped to action ranges
Continuous actions are not possible with quantum policies

questions.explanation: Continuous actions can be obtained by measuring expectation values like ⟨Z_i⟩ (in [-1,1]) and mapping them to the desired action range.

questions.question: What is a key challenge for training quantum policies?

options.text: Shot noise from measurements increases gradient variance
Quantum policies always output uniform distributions

questions.explanation: Finite measurement shots introduce stochastic noise in probability estimates and gradients, requiring variance reduction techniques like baseline subtraction.

questions.question: Which are valid state encoding strategies? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Dense encoding with multiple rotations per qubit

questions.explanation: All are valid: (a) angle encoding uses rotations, (b) amplitude encoding puts features in amplitudes, (c) IQP adds feature products, (d) dense encoding uses RY and RZ per qubit.
